title: Tipping Your Skipper and Crew: A Simple Guide
tags: yacht charter, boat rental, tipping, travel, sailing
topic: Tipping your skipper and crew: a simple guide
html:
You have had a wonderful day on the water. The skipper found the quiet cove, the deckhand kept the drinks cold and the swim ladder ready, and nobody had to think about navigation or anchoring once. Then the boat noses back into the marina and a small, slightly awkward question surfaces: how much do you tip the crew, and how do you actually hand it over without fumbling. It is one of the most common worries among people new to chartering, and the good news is that the answer is far simpler than the nervous chatter online suggests. This is a plain, practical guide to tipping at sea, written for renters anywhere in the world.

Why crew tips work differently to a restaurant

On most crewed charters the day rate you pay covers the boat, the fuel arrangement, the insurance and the basic running of the trip. It does not usually fold in a service charge for the people actually looking after you. A professional skipper and crew often earn a modest base wage and rely on tips to round out their season, in much the same way as staff in hospitality do. So a gratuity is not a tax or a trick. It is the normal way guests recognise good work, and crews remember the boats and the guests who treat them well. When you rent a boat with a skipper, it helps to budget for this from the start rather than scrambling for cash at the end.

A simple rule of thumb

If you want one number to anchor your thinking, the widely used custom is to tip somewhere between five and twenty per cent of the charter price, with ten to fifteen per cent sitting comfortably in the middle for good service. Adjust within that range based on how the day actually went.

  • Around five to ten per cent for a pleasant trip where everything was fine and professional.
  • Ten to fifteen per cent for a crew who clearly went out of their way, anticipated what you needed, and made the day memorable.
  • Fifteen to twenty per cent for genuinely exceptional service on a special occasion, a long charter, or a large, demanding group.

For a short day hire of a smaller boat, a flat amount often makes more sense than a percentage. A note or two per guest handed to a friendly skipper is a warm and perfectly judged gesture. The maths matters less than the thought behind it.

How to hand it over

This is the part people overthink. Cash in the local currency is still king at sea, partly because it is shared out easily and partly because card tips can take an age to filter through. Bring it in an envelope, hand it to the skipper at the end of the trip, and say a sincere thank you. On a multi-crew yacht the convention is to give the whole tip to the captain, who then divides it fairly among the team. You do not need to work out individual shares yourself.

A quiet word, an envelope, and a genuine thank you will say more than any awkward speech at the dock.

If you book through a marketplace and want to plan ahead, you can ask about tipping norms when you arrange the boat rental, since customs vary a little by country. In some destinations a service element is more commonly included, and a quick question up front saves any guesswork later.

When a tip is optional

If you have hired a bareboat with no crew, there is nobody to tip, so that worry disappears entirely. The same goes if you take the helm yourself on a small open boat without a skipper. Tipping is also genuinely optional rather than obligatory. If the service was poor, the crew was rude, or safety was handled carelessly, you are under no duty to reward it, and honest feedback to the operator is more useful than a grudging tip. Most of the time, though, the crew has worked hard in the heat to give you a brilliant day, and a fair gratuity is the easiest way to close the loop. Whenever you hire a boat with people aboard to look after you, factor a little extra into your overall budget so the moment never catches you out.

A note for boat owners

If you are on the other side of this and you own a vessel, tips can be part of what makes crewing appealing to the skippers you work with, and a happy crew keeps guests coming back. Owners who decide to list your boat on a worldwide platform can set clear expectations about service and gratuities in the listing, which keeps everyone comfortable and avoids surprises on the day.

Tipping your skipper and crew really does come down to a short checklist: carry some local cash, aim for ten to fifteen per cent when the day is good, hand it to the captain with a real thank you, and adjust up or down for the experience you actually had. Do that, and the only thing left to remember about your day at sea will be the water, the sun, and how easy the whole thing felt.
